details.text: Derivative: أَلْهَا is أَلْهَى – يُلْهِي – إِلْهَاء which comes from the original trilateral root is لَهْو (Lahw). لَهْو (Lahw) means entertainment or amusement. It refers to something that keeps you busy and takes you away from something that is supposed to be done i.e., a diversion or distraction. أَلْهَى means to be distracted and its usage implies that the matter someone is being distracted by and occupying himself in is less important and beneficial than the matter you were distracted from i.e., to be preoccupied by that which is less important and less beneficial.

Derivative: ٱلتَّكَاثُرُ comes from the word كَثْرَة (Kathrah) which means plentifulness. ٱلتَّكَاثُرُ (At-Takaathur) is from the تَفَاعُل verb pattern form and it can imply four meanings: 1) The desire to have a lot and to have more and more, 2) Competing with others in attaining a lot, 3) To have pride in having more and 4) That the desire to want more is a common and collectively shared urge.

أَلْهَاكُمُ التَّكَاثُرُ ‎

- “The prideful mutual pursuit of piling up wealth (التَّكَاثُرُ) has distracted you (أَلْهَاكُمُ)”. The statement states that “you have been distracted” but it doesn’t state what has been distracted from. As such it has been left unrestricted and has been said that you have been distracted “from the obedience to Allah or the Qur’an or working for the hereafter etc”.
Derivative: زُرْتُمُ from زَارَ – زِيَارَة means to meet and visit someone and in classical Arabic it meant to hug someone. A زِيَارَة (Ziyaarah - visit) is temporary.
Derivative: ٱلْمَقَابِرَ is the plural of مَقْبَرَة (Maqbarah) which is a graveyard.

حَتَّىٰ زُرْتُمُ الْمَقَابِرَ ‎

- “Until (حَتَّىٰ) you have visited (زُرْتُمُ) the graves or graveyards (الْمَقَابِرَ)”. This is an expression to mean “until death reaches you”. Another interpreted meaning is: “You even visited the graves”. This second meaning implies that you used to visit the graves but took no heed from that.

Derivative: كَلَّا is according to the meaning of حَقًّا (Haqqan – Truly or for sure). كَلَّا (Kallaa) literally means: “Not at all” or “No way” with the implication that this delusion of yours will not last and you will be made to see the true reality.
Derivative: سَوْفَ is used to indicate to the near future i.e., something “will” happen. سَوْفَ (Sawfa) implies a time closer to the future than سَ (Sa).

ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ

- “Then (again or after) (ثُمَّ), truly (كَلَّا) you will soon (سَوْفَ) come to know (تَعْلَمُونَ)”. This repetition of the previous verse could be for emphasis to emphasise the warning. The additional ثُمَّ (Thumma) can also add emphasis to the statement with the implication of: I’m stating it “again”. Or the ثُمَّ (Thumma – then and after) in the second verse can imply that this refers to “coming to know” for a second occasion, where for instance the first relates to when you reach the graves and the second when you are extracted from them on the Day of Judgement.
Derivative: لَوْ (Law) is a word that refer to regret (حَسْرَة - Hasrah) and implies the meaning of: “If only” or “Had”.
Derivative: الْيَقِينِ refers to an absolute conviction.

- عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ (‘Ilm ul-Yaqeen) literally means “The knowledge of certainty”. It refers to knowledge that is based upon certainty. It does not require that something be seen like smoke is knowledge that indicates with certainty that there is a fire even if that fire is not seen. There are two other expressions with الْيَقِينِ mentioned in the Qur’an: عَيْنُ اليَقين (‘Ain ul-Yaqeen) which is certainty based on what you see with your own eyes and حَقُّ اليَقِين (Haqq ul-Yaqeen)) which is certainty that goes beyond the certainty of what your eyes can see and reflects the highest level of absolute certainty like when the truth is affirmed by all senses.
Derivative: الْجَحِيمَ comes from جُحْم (Juhm) which is the paralysing or petrifying stare of the lion before it attacks its prey. It is the name that has been given for hellfire.

لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ

- Concerning لَتَرَوُنَّ: The لَام (Laam - لَ) that has been attached to تَرَوُ (you will see) is لَام التَّوْكيد (Laam At-Tawkeed – The Laam of emphasis). The use of this لَام (Laam - لَ) here implies the presence of a قَسْم (Qasm - oath). The double نُونُ (Noon) at the end of تَرَوُ (you will see) represents an extra double emphasis. As such the verb تَرَوُ (you will see) has three levels of emphasis attached to it.
Derivative: ثُمَّ does not only imply the meaning of then in terms of after but can also imply “moreover” to emphasise a matter which is what is intended here.
- عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ literally means “the eye of certainty” and means the conviction based upon what you see with your own eyes.

Derivative: النَّعِيمِ is called a صِفَة مُشَبَّهَة (Sifah Mushabbahah) as it has a يَاء (Yaa - ي) in it upon the pattern form of فَعِيل. It refers to the blessings and favours in terms of comforts and luxuries which the person had during his life. النَّعِيمِ because it has a يَاء (Yaa - ي) in it upon the pattern form of فَعِيل indicates that it is a constant blessing i.e., those blessings that you enjoyed consistently.
Derivative: ثُمَّ here means “then” i.e., after what preceded it.

ثُمَّ لَتُسْأَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ

- Concerning ثُمَّ لَتُسْأَلُنَّ: This statement contains four levels of emphasis: (1) ثُمَّ (Thumma – Moreover) (2) لَام التَّوْكيد (Laam At-Tawkeed – The Laam of emphasis) and (3 and 4) The double نُونُ (Noon) which adds two levels of emphasis in the case where a single additional نُونُ (Noon) adds one extra level of emphasis. The meaning is then something like: “You will then moreover assuredly without doubt surely be asked or questioned”.
